content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 22, 2011

Louis B. Green
County Counsel
El Dorado County 330 Fair lane
Placerville, CA 95667

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-11-209

Dear Mr. Green:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct
that may have already taken place, and any conclusions provided apply only to prospective
actions.

QUESTION

May Supervisor Nutting participate in decisions involving amendments to the General
Plan and to the zoning ordinance governing the Timber Production Zone District ("TPZ"), to
potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone district?

CONCLUSION

No. Supervisor Nutting may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions
regarding the proposed amendments to the General Plan and the zoning ordinance governing the
TPZ to potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone district because the
governmental decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real
property interests. In addition, based on the facts you have provided, we are unable to apply the
public generally exception to his situation.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-209
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are County Counsel for the County of El Dorado. You seek formal written advice
on behalf of Supervisor Ray Nutting, who is supervisor of the Second District, with respect to his
duties under the Act.

The El Dorado Board of Supervisors is scheduled to vote to initiate amendments to the
General Plan and to the Zoning Ordinance that implements the General Plan. One proposed
change to the zoning ordinance, which the Board's advisory committee has recommended, is a
proposed amendment to the ordinance governing the TPZ, to potentially modify the limit on
single family residences in that zone district.

Currently, a residential dwelling is only allowed upon approval of a conditional use
permit and only if specific findings are made about the demonstrated need for a house to assist in
"intensive management" of the timber.

While the language of the proposed zoning ordinance amendment has not been
developed, the gist of the proposed change would be to make it easier, to the maximum extent
allowable under Government Code Section 51104(h)(6), to obtain a permit for a single family
residence on a parcel zoned TPZ, including potentially removing the requirement of a
conditional use permit. This proposed change to the zoning ordinance would apply to all TPZ
zoned land in the County.

Supervisor Nutting is the owner of three adjacent parcels zoned TPZ. Two of the parcels
(137 acres and 80 acres in size) already have homes on them and would not be affected by the
proposed amendments. However, the third parcel, which is 60 acres in size, is currently
undeveloped. This parcel would not be eligible for a permit for a residence under the current
ordinance because Supervisor Nutting could not demonstrate that the additional residence on that
parcel is necessary for the intensive management of the timber on the three jointly managed
parcels. Under the proposed amendments it is possible the property could be developed with a
residence without the requirement of a conditional use permit.

In our telephone conversation of November 14, 2011, you stated that the Board is
reviewing proposals to amend the zoning ordinance for additional "residential or recreational
use." You stated that currently the third parcel is useable for timberland purposes and that a
change in the zoning ordinance "could add value" to the property, its development potential or
income producing potential due to the possible development of a single family home. It is
currently unknown what impact additional "recreational use" of the property would have under
the proposal being considered. However, you did say it could change the character of the
neighborhood, or results in "more use or traffic' in the area.

There are 92,800 privately owned parcels in the county and 863 are zoned TPZ. In
Supervisor Nutting's district there are 18,766 private owned parcels and 278 are zoned TPZ.
You do not have the number of "property owners" in the county or the number of "property

File No. A-11-209
Page No. 3

owners" who own TPZ zoned property. However, you estimate that "less than 1% of [real
property] ownership" in the county would be impacted by the decisions.

You wish to know whether Supervisor Nutting has a conflict of interest that would
disqualify him from participating in the decision to initiate the proposed change to the zoning
ordinance, and subsequently from voting on that change. You also wish to know if Supervisor
Nutting has a conflict of interest in the decisions, whether he may nonetheless participate
through application of the "public generally" exception.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest."
(Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member
of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests.

Under the Act, a conflict of interest exists only when a public official has a financial
interest in a particular governmental decision. To determine whether a public official has a
"conflict of interest" in a specific governmental decision, we employ a standard eight-step
analysis outlined at subdivisions 1 through 8 of Regulation 18700(b).

Your facts acknowledge that Supervisor Nutting is a public official who will be making,
participating in making, or will be using his official position to influence a governmental decision.
You also identify his real property interest in three adjacent parcels zoned TPZ.

Because your question centers on whether Supervisor Nutting's economic interest in real
property is directly or indirectly involved and what the material standard is for determining a
conflict in this situation, we begin our analysis at Step 4.

Step Four: Is Supervisor Nutting's economic interest directly or indirectly involved
in the decision?

To determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on an economic interest is material or not, you first must determine whether the interest is
directly or indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).)

For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the standards set forth in
Regulation 18704.2 apply.

Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:

File No. A-11-209
Page No. 4

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision...

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property ...

"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use
or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest.

"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property
in which the official has an interest.

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in
which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services."

However, Regulation 18704.2 (b) further provides:

"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a governmental
decision, but is instead indirectly involved if:

"(1) The decision solely concerns the amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as changes in the uses
permitted, or development standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in that
category, which shall be analyzed under 2 Cal. Code Regs. section
18705.2(b) . . .."

File No. A-11-209
Page No. 5

Ordinarily, Supervisor Nutting's properties would be deemed directly involved in the
decisions because his properties are subject to the zoning amendment decisions. However,
because the governmental decisions solely concern the amendment of existing regulations
applicable to all other properties designated in that category as provided in the exception above,
Supervisor Nutting's properties are considered indirectly involved in the governmental decisions.

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)

Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each of his
or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that
the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to
be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra, at 198.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that
must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.

Indirectly Involved Real Property:

The financial effect of a governmental decision on indirectly involved real property is
presumed not to be material. However, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest that make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which
the public official has an interest. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)

You stated that the Board is reviewing proposals to amend the zoning ordinance for
additional "residential or recreational use" and that the decisions "could likely add value" to the
property' because of the possibility of building a single family home on the parcel, a factor that
would increase its development potential and income producing potential. Your facts indicate
that under the current ordinance it is highly unlikely that a home could be built on the site. In
addition, you stated that while it is currently unknown what impact additional "recreational use"
of the property would have under the proposal, you acknowledge that it could change the
character of the neighborhood, or result in "more use or traffic' in the area.

File No. A-11-209
Page No. 6


Because the development potential or income producing potential of Supervisor Nutting's
property would likely be impacted by the decision to amend the General Plan and zoning
ordinance with respect to TPZ properties, it appears reasonably foreseeable that the decisions
will have a material financial effect on his economic interest in real property.

Therefore, Supervisor Nutting may not make, participate in making, or influence
decisions regarding the proposed amendments to the General Plan and the zoning ordinance
governing the TPZ, to potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone
district.

**Step Seven: Public Generally**

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

*Public Generally: Basic Rule*

Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interests will experience a
material financial effect as a result of the governmental decision before the official, he or she
may still participate under the "public generally" exception if the material financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is indistinguishable from its
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707.)

Therefore, Supervisor Nutting may participate in the governmental decision in which he
has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest only if he meets the requirements of the basic public
generally exception under Regulation 18707.1.

Under the basic public generally rule, Supervisor Nutting must meet a two-part test by
showing that the decision would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantial the
same manner" as it financially affects his economic interest.

For decisions that affect a public official's real property, "significant segment" is defined
as 10-percent or more of all property owners or all residential property owners in El Dorado
County, or 5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the county. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1)(B).)

The facts you provided indicate that it is unlikely that the above thresholds will be met, as
you estimated that "less than 1% of ownership" in the county would be affected by the decisions.
Therefore, the analysis ends because the first prong of a two-part test set forth in Regulation
18707.1(b) is not met.

File No. A-11-209
Page No. 7

Step Eight: Legally Required Participation

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section
87101; Regulation 18708.)

Because you have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required participation
exception is applicable here, we do not analyze this exception.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jgl

May Supervisor Nutting participate in decisions involving amendments to the General 
Plan and to the zoning ordinance governing the Timber Production Zone District ("TPZ"), to 
potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone district?

No. Supervisor Nutting may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions

public generally ex(cption to his situation.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." 
(Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material

of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests. 
Under the Act, a conflict of interest exists only when a public official has a financial 
interest in a particular governmental decision. To determine whether a public official has a 
"conflict of interest" in a specific governmental decision, we employ a standard eight-step 
analysis outlined at subdivisions 1 through 8 of Regulation 18700(b). 
Your facts acknowledge that Supervisor Nutting is a public official who will be making, 
participating in making, or will be using his official position to influence a government decision. 
You also identify his real property interest in three adjacent parcels zoned TPZ. 
Because your question centers on whether Supervisor Nutting's economic interest in real 
property is directly or indirectly involved and what the material standard is for determining a 
conflict in this situation, we begin our analysis at Step 4. 
Step Four: Is Supervisor Nutting's economic interest directly or indirectly involved 
in the decision? 
To determine whether a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
on an economic interest is material or not, you first must determine whether the interest is 
directly or indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704(a).) 
For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the standards set forth in 
Regulation 18704.2 apply. 
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an economic 
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:  
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of 
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries ( or the 
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the 
governmental decision ... 
"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, 
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or 
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental 
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a 
similar decision affecting the real property ... 
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation 
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use 
or uses of the real property in which the official has an interest. 
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or 
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property 
in which the official has an interest. 
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select 
the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area 
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the 
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to 
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which 
the <;>fficial has an interest, or any part of it is located within the 
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area. 
"(6) The decision involves construction of or improvements to, streets, 
water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in 
which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services." 
However, Regulation 18704.2 (b) further provides: 
"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a 
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a governmental 
decision, but is instead indirectly involved if: 
"( 1) The decision solely concđrns the amendment of an existing zon'rng 
ordinance or other land use regulation ( such as changes in the uses 
permitted, or development standards applicable, within a particular zoning 
category) which is applicable to all other properties designated in that 
category, which shall be analyzed under 2 Cal. Code Regs. section 
18705.2(b) . ... "  
Ordinarily, Supervisor Nutting's properties would be deemed directly involved in the 
decisions because his properties are subject to the zoning amendment decisions. However, 
because the governmental decisions solely concern the amendment of existing zoning regulations 
applicable to all other properties designated in that category as provided in the exception above, 
Supervisor Nutting's properties are considered indirectly involved in the governmental decisions. 
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on your economic interests? 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a 
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation 
18700(a).) 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each of his 
or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that 
the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably 
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met 
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to 
be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re 
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is 
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra, at 198.) 
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby 
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that 
must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official. 
Indirectly Involved Real Property: 
The financial effect of a governmental decision on indirectly involved real property is 
presumed not to be material. However, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are 
specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of 
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest that make it reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which 
the public official has an interest. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(l).) 
You stated that the Board is reviewing proposals to amend the zoning ordinance for 
additional "residential or recreational use" and that the decisions "could likely add value" to the 
property" because of the pos;'tbility of building a single family home on the parcel, a factor that 
would increase its development potential and income producing potential. Your facts indicate 
that under the current ordinance it is highly unlikely that a home could be built on the site. In 
addition, you stated that while it is currently unknown what impact additional "recreational use" 
of the property would have under the proposal, you acknowledge that it could change the 
character of the neighborhood, or result in "more use or traffic' in the area.  
Because the development potential or income producing potential of Supervisor Nutting' s 
property would likely be impacted by the decision to amend the General Plan and zoning 
ordinance with respect to TPZ properties, it appears reasonably foreseeable that the decisions 
will have a material financial effect on his economic interest in real property. 
Therefore, Supervisor Nutting may not make, participate in making, or influence 
decisions regarding the proposed amendments to the General Plan and the zoning ordinance 
governing the TPZ, to potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone 
district. 
Step Seven: Public Generally 
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the 
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is 
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. 
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) 
Public Generally: Basic Rule 
Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interests will experience a 
material financial effect as a result of the governmental decision before the official, he or she 
may still participate under the "public generally" exception if the material financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is indistinguishable from its 
effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707.) 
Therefore, Supervisor Nutting may participate in the governmental decision in which he 
has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest only if he meets the requirements of the basic public 
generally exception under Regulation 18707.1. 
Under the basic public generally rule, Supervisor Nutting must meet a two-part test by 
showing that the decision would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantial the 
same manner" as it financially affects his economic interest. 
For decisions that affect a public official's real property, "significant segment" is defined 
as IO-percent or more of all property owners or all residential property owners in El Dorado 
County, or 5,000 property owners or residential property owners in the county. (Regulation 
18707.1 (b )( 1 )(B ).) 
The facts you provided indicate that it is unlikely that the above thresholds will be met, as 
you estimated that "less than 1 % of ownership" in the county would be affected by the decisions. 
Therefore, the analysis ends because the first prong of a two-part test set forth in Regulation 
18707.l(b) is not met. 


Step Eight: Legally Required Participation  
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take 
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very 
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 
87101; Regulation 18708.) 
Because you have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required participation 
exception is applicable here, we do not analyze this exception.

Plan and to the zoning ordinance governing the Timber Production Zone District ("TPZ"), to 
potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone district?

CONCLUSION: No. Supervisor Nutting may not make, participate in making, or influence decisions 
regarding the proposed amendments to the General Plan and the zoning ordinance governing the 
TPZ to potentially modify the limit on single family residences in that zone district because the 
governmental decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real 
property interests. In addition, based on the facts you have provided, we are unable to apply the 
public generally ex(cption to his situation.
